info.title: PayTraces — Crypto Forensics API
info.description:
PayTraces is a crypto-forensics API for EVM-compatible blockchains (Ethereum mainnet today; Tron and others coming). It builds a searchable graph of on-chain value transfers around an address, traces tainted funds to their final sinks, and produces a per-address risk score with explainable evidence.

---

## What you can do with this API

- **Reconstruct the transfer graph** around any wallet — incoming and outgoing native + ERC-20 edges, paginated, with BFS depth and node-count caps you control.
- **Trace tainted funds forward or backward** through multiple hops using FIFO, LIFO, Haircut, or Poison strategies — useful for AML investigations after a theft, hack, or sanctioned counterparty interaction.
- **Score an address for risk** by aggregating signals from entity labels, sink exposure (mixer / sanctioned / darknet), and behavioural heuristics. Returns 0 (clean) to 100 (critical) with a list of contributing signals.
- **Screen against sanctions lists** (OFAC / EU / UN) with a single GET. Bulk variant for batch checks.
- **Detect cluster-formation patterns** — fan-in / fan-out / peeling chain / smurfing cycle / temporal burst / fixed-amount clustering / dwell time. Each detector produces evidence with the matching counterparties and a confidence band.
- **Manage entity labels** (admin) — attach exchange / mixer / sanctioned / scam / bridge / darknet labels to addresses so downstream scoring and tracing benefit from your private attribution data.

---

## Authentication

Two independent headers protect different parts of the API:

| Header | When required | Endpoints |
|--------|---------------|-----------|
| `X-Api-Key` | Set on the server (optional) | All `/graph`, `/score`, `/sanctions`, `/trace`, `/heuristics`, ... |
| `X-Admin-Api-Key` | Set on the server (optional) | Mutation endpoints: `POST /labels`, `POST /entities`, `POST /watchlist`, `POST /address/.../kind` |
| `Authorization: Bearer <key>` | Alternative to `X-Api-Key` | Same as above |

If the server has no API key configured, the corresponding headers are NOT required. The Scalar "Authentication" panel (top-right of this UI) lets you set both headers once per session.

---

## API versioning

Every request MUST carry an `X-API-Version: 1` header. Without it the server returns `HTTP 400 missing required header`. With an unsupported value it returns the same status with the supported version listed. Only `/scalar` (this UI) and `/api-docs/openapi.json` (the raw spec) are exempt.

This is a deliberately strict policy: it makes breaking schema changes safe to introduce on a new version while old clients keep working on `v1`.

---

## End-to-end workflow

Most use cases follow the same shape: ingest first, then read.

**Step 1.** Schedule ingestion for an address. This is async — it returns immediately with a job id while the worker walks the on-chain history and writes counterparty transfers to PostgreSQL.
```bash
curl -X POST http://localhost:8080/jobs/ingest \
-H 'X-API-Version: 1' \
-H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
-d '{
"address": "0xd8dA6BF26964aF9D7eEd9e03E53415D37aA96045",
"chain_id": 1,
"max_depth": 3,
"max_nodes": 500
}'
# → { "job_id": "01HX...", "status": "queued" }
```

**Step 2.** Poll job status until it succeeds.
```bash
curl 'http://localhost:8080/jobs/01HX...' \
-H 'X-API-Version: 1'
# → { "status": "succeeded", "finished_at": ... }
```

**Step 3.** Read the transfer graph from the DB (this never touches a chain source — pure read).
```bash
curl 'http://localhost:8080/graph?address=0xd8dA...&chain_id=1&max_depth=2&page=0&page_size=100' \
-H 'X-API-Version: 1'
```

**Step 4.** Score and screen.
```bash
curl 'http://localhost:8080/score?address=0xd8dA...&chain_id=1' \
-H 'X-API-Version: 1'
# → { "score": 42, "signals": [...] }
curl 'http://localhost:8080/sanctions?address=0xd8dA...&chain_id=1' \
-H 'X-API-Version: 1'
```

**Step 5.** Inspect behavioural heuristics — fan-in, fan-out, smurfing, peeling, etc.
```bash
curl 'http://localhost:8080/heuristics?address=0xd8dA...&chain_id=1' \
-H 'X-API-Version: 1'
```

Or follow the money:
```bash
curl 'http://localhost:8080/trace?address=0xd8dA...&chain_id=1&direction=forward&strategy=haircut&max_hops=5' \
-H 'X-API-Version: 1'
```

---

## Architecture

- **Chain sources.** Per-chain, configurable. For Ethereum: Etherscan, Alchemy, Moralis, BigQuery, or a `routed` orchestrator that fails over between them on rate-limits. For Tron: TronGrid. Set via the `ethereum.source:` / `tron.source:` block in `config.yaml`.
- **Storage.** PostgreSQL holds transfers, entity labels, address kinds (EOA / Contract / KnownService), watchlists, alerts. Read endpoints (`/graph`, `/score`, ...) hit only the DB, so they never block on a chain RPC.
- **Ingestion.** `POST /jobs/ingest` enqueues a worker that walks the address graph BFS, fetches transfers from the configured chain source, persists them, and classifies counterparties as EOA vs. contract. Rate limits and retries are handled inside the source layer.
- **Risk model.** `GET /score` aggregates RiskSignals (entity labels + sink exposure from forward/backward Haircut traces) using a configurable strategy (`max` or `weighted_count` with dedup). Tunables live under the `score:` block in `config.yaml`.
- **Heuristics.** Cluster-formation detectors (fan-in/out, peeling, smurfing, burst, fixed-amount, dwell) feed into `GET /heuristics` and `POST /cluster`. Thresholds and windows live under the `heuristics:` block in `config.yaml`.

---

## Pagination

`GET /graph` is paginated by edges. `nodes` is returned only on `page == 0` because the node set is global per request — paginate through edges using `page` + `page_size` (default 100, max 1000).

Other list endpoints (`/labels`, `/watchlist`, `/alerts`) return the full collection in one response — these are admin endpoints expected to stay small.

---

## Common errors

| Status | Meaning |
|--------|---------|
| `400 Bad Request` | Missing/invalid `X-API-Version`, malformed body, unknown chain id, address that doesn't parse for the chain family. |
| `401 Unauthorized` | `X-Api-Key` or `X-Admin-Api-Key` missing or wrong. |
| `404 Not Found` | Job id / entity id / label / watchlist entry not found. |
| `409 Conflict` | Duplicate label or duplicate watchlist entry. |
| `500 Internal Server Error` | Database, chain source, or internal bug. Response body carries the message. |

All errors share the `ErrorResponse` schema documented below.

get.description:
Look up the address-kind classification (EOA / Contract / KnownService).

## What this does

Returns whatever classification the server currently holds for the address. The kind is set either by the ingestion classifier (which detects EOA vs. contract from on-chain code) or by an explicit admin override via `POST /address/{addr}/kind`. Addresses the classifier has never seen come back as `unknown`.

This is a fast DB-only read; no chain RPC is touched.

## When to use it

Use this when rendering an address in a UI to pick the right icon (wallet vs. contract vs. named service), or to gate features that only make sense for EOAs.

## Example

```bash
curl 'http://localhost:8080/address/0xd8dA6BF26964aF9D7eEd9e03E53415D37aA96045/kind' \
-H 'X-API-Version: 1'
```

## Notes

The path parameter is parsed against Ethereum mainnet (`chain_id = 1`) — non-EVM addresses use the same route only as long as their canonical form is accepted by the parser.

parameters.name: addr
parameters.description: Address in hex (EVM) or canonical chain form (other families).

200.description: Current classification. `kind == "unknown"` is the default for unseen addresses; `service_name` is non-null only when `kind == "known_service"`.

400.description: Path parameter failed to parse as an address.

post.description:
Override the address-kind classification for an address (admin only).

## What this does

Writes the supplied kind/service_name pair into the address-kinds table, replacing any prior classification. Useful when the automatic classifier has nothing to go on (e.g. an account that has not yet emitted code) or when you want to attach a friendly service name (`known_service`).

The new value takes effect immediately for all subsequent reads — the classifier honours admin overrides.

## When to use it

Use this for curated allow/deny lists of named services (exchanges, payment processors). For pure risk labels (mixer / sanctioned / scam), prefer `POST /labels` — the categorisation there carries a richer model.

## Example

```bash
curl -X POST 'http://localhost:8080/address/0xd8dA6BF26964aF9D7eEd9e03E53415D37aA96045/kind' \
-H 'X-API-Version: 1' \
-H 'X-Admin-Api-Key: <admin-key>' \
-H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
-d '{"kind": "known_service", "service_name": "Binance hot wallet", "chain_id": 1}'
```

## Notes

Requires the `X-Admin-Api-Key` header. `kind` must be one of `eoa`, `contract`, `known_service`, `unknown` — anything else triggers a 400.

parameters.name: addr
parameters.description: Address in hex (EVM) or canonical chain form.

200.description: Updated classification (echoes the stored row).

400.description: Address failed to parse, or `kind` is not one of the allowed values.

get.description:
Return the alert audit log (admin only).

## What this does

Reads every alert previously written by ingestion runs that touched a watchlisted address. Each entry records the watched address, the specific triggering transfer, the timestamp, and the watchlist reason as of the moment the alert was raised.

This endpoint is the audit-log counterpart to `/watchlist`: the watchlist defines *what to watch for*, and this endpoint shows *what has happened*.

## When to use it

Poll periodically from a monitoring dashboard, or read on demand when investigating an incident. Removing an address from `/watchlist` does NOT remove existing alerts — they remain for audit purposes.

## Example

```bash
curl 'http://localhost:8080/alerts' \
-H 'X-API-Version: 1' \
-H 'X-Admin-Api-Key: <admin-key>'
```

## Notes

Requires the `X-Admin-Api-Key` header. No pagination — the response includes the full log. If the volume grows large, prune it directly in the database.

200.description: Every alert ever recorded by ingestion, oldest first. May be empty.

get.description:
List every blockchain the server knows about and whether it is operational.

## What this does

Returns the full chain registry — one entry per supported chain — together with a flag (`source_registered`) indicating whether a live data source is configured for that chain on this server. Use this endpoint to discover which `chain_id` values are valid in other endpoints and which of those chains can actually serve traffic right now.

This is a pure in-memory read (no DB, no chain RPC), so it is cheap to call from a client at startup or for health-style checks.

## When to use it

Call this once at client startup to populate a chain picker, or whenever you receive a `400` complaining about an unknown chain id. A chain with `source_registered = false` is in the registry but cannot ingest or read data — calls passing its `chain_id` will fail.

## Example

```bash
curl 'http://localhost:8080/chains' \
-H 'X-API-Version: 1'
```

## Notes

The set of registered chains is fixed for the lifetime of the server process and is driven by `config.yaml` (the presence of `ethereum:` / `tron:` blocks and configured source credentials).

200.description: Full list of registered chains. Each entry carries the numeric chain id, family, native asset metadata, confirmation depth, and a `source_registered` flag distinguishing live chains from registry-only entries.

get.description:
Group an address with its likely co-owned siblings using Union-Find over every heuristic.

## What this does

Runs every cluster-formation detector (fan-in, fan-out, smurfing cycle, peeling chain, deposit-address reuse, ...) over the persisted graph around the address and unions each detector's outputs into connected components using Union-Find. The result is a partition of the discovered addresses into clusters that probably share a real-world owner.

This endpoint reads only from the DB. The queried address is always returned in `components[0]`; other components describe satellite clusters discovered during traversal.

## When to use it

Use this after ingesting an address (`POST /jobs/ingest`) to expand a single suspect into the wider set of addresses that probably belong to the same actor. Complementary to `/heuristics` (which says *why*) — this endpoint says *with whom*.

## Example

```bash
curl 'http://localhost:8080/cluster?address=0xd8dA6BF26964aF9D7eEd9e03E53415D37aA96045&chain_id=1' \
-H 'X-API-Version: 1'
```

## Notes

Component contents are heuristic — they are an *upper bound* on co-ownership, not a proof. Larger components on whales/exchanges are expected; treat the first component as the primary answer.

200.description: Connected-component partition. `components[0]` always contains the queried address. An empty `components` array means no co-ownership signals were detected (rare unless ingestion produced no graph).

400.description: Address failed to parse or `chain_id` is unknown.

500.description: Database read or detector failure. The error is in the body.

get.description:
Rank the transfers around an address by forensic significance.

## What this does

Loads every incoming and outgoing transfer for `address` from the persisted graph, scores each one with the edge-significance heuristic (which considers amount, timing, counterparty rarity, and contextual outliers among the subject's other edges), and returns the top `limit` highest-scoring edges.

This is a DB-only read. It is a complement to `/heuristics` and `/cluster`: where those summarise patterns, this surfaces the specific transactions an investigator should look at first.

## When to use it

Use this as the second step in manual triage of an address: get the score with `/score`, then pull the most significant edges here to ground the investigation in concrete transactions. Run `POST /jobs/ingest` first.

## Example

```bash
curl 'http://localhost:8080/edges/significance?address=0xd8dA6BF26964aF9D7eEd9e03E53415D37aA96045&chain_id=1&limit=20' \
-H 'X-API-Version: 1'
```

## Notes

The score is heuristic and not directly comparable across different subject addresses — it is calibrated to the subject's own neighbourhood. `limit` defaults to 50.

parameters.name: limit
parameters.description: How many top-scored edges to return (default 50).

200.description: Top-N edges sorted by descending significance score. An empty `edges` array means no transfers have been ingested for this address yet.

400.description: Address failed to parse or `chain_id` is unknown.

500.description: Database read failure. The error message is in the body.

200.description: Entity created

400.description: Invalid category

parameters.name: id
parameters.description: Entity UUID

200.description: Entity details

parameters.name: id
parameters.description: Entity UUID

200.description: Updated entity

400.description: Invalid address or unknown entity

parameters.name: id
parameters.description: Entity UUID

parameters.name: addr
parameters.description: Address (hex or canonical)

200.description: Address removed

400.description: Invalid address or unknown entity

get.description:
Read the persisted transfer graph around an address, paginated by edge.

## What this does

Walks the persisted graph in PostgreSQL outward from `address`, BFS-bounded by `max_depth` hops and `max_nodes` distinct counterparties, then returns the discovered transfers paginated by edge. This endpoint never contacts a chain RPC; it is a pure read against whatever ingestion has already persisted, so the response is fast and deterministic but bounded by what has already been ingested.

## When to use it

Run `POST /jobs/ingest` for the same address first and wait for the job to reach `succeeded`. Then call this endpoint to render the graph, drive a visualisation, or feed it into downstream tooling. If you call it before ingestion, you will get an empty graph rather than an error.

## Example

```bash
curl 'http://localhost:8080/graph?address=0xd8dA6BF26964aF9D7eEd9e03E53415D37aA96045&chain_id=1&max_depth=2&page=0&page_size=100' \
-H 'X-API-Version: 1'
```

## Notes

The `nodes` array is only returned on `page == 0` because the node set is global per request; subsequent pages send an empty `nodes` array to save bandwidth. `page_size` is clamped to `[1, 1000]`. The `from_block` / `to_block` filters apply to the persisted edges only.

parameters.name: address
parameters.description: Origin wallet address

parameters.name: chain_id
parameters.description: Chain ID. Defaults to 1 (Ethereum mainnet).

parameters.name: max_depth
parameters.description: How many hops to traverse from the origin. Defaults to 3.

parameters.name: max_nodes
parameters.description: Hard cap on the total number of nodes in the graph. Defaults to 500.

parameters.name: max_transfers_per_address
parameters.description: Per-address cap on transfers fetched from the chain/repo. Defaults to 10000.

parameters.name: from_block
parameters.description: Restrict transfers to blocks ≥ this height (inclusive).

parameters.name: to_block
parameters.description: Restrict transfers to blocks ≤ this height (inclusive).

parameters.name: page
parameters.description: Page index (0-based). Defaults to 0. Nodes are returned only on `page == 0`.

parameters.name: page_size
parameters.description: Number of edges per page. Defaults to 100, maximum 1000.

200.description: One page of the persisted graph. `total_nodes` / `total_edges` describe the full result set; `edges` carries the current page only; `nodes` is non-empty only on page 0. An empty graph (no nodes, no edges) means ingestion has not produced data for this address yet — run `POST /jobs/ingest`.

400.description: Address could not be parsed for the chain family, an unknown `chain_id` was supplied, or numeric parameters (page, page_size, max_depth, etc.) were out of range.

500.description: Database read failure. The original error message is included in the body for debugging.

get.description:
Run every behavioural-pattern detector against an address and report what fired.

## What this does

Evaluates eight independent cluster-formation heuristics against the persisted transfers around `address`: fan-out, fan-in, smurfing cycle, temporal burst, fixed-amount clustering, dwell-time pass-through, peeling chain, and deposit-address reuse. Each detector independently returns an evidence object (with the participating counterparties and a confidence band) or `null` when its pattern did not match.

All thresholds — minimum fan-out/fan-in counts, burst multipliers, window lengths, BFS depth caps — come from the `heuristics:` block in `config.yaml`. This endpoint is DB-only; no chain RPC is touched.

## When to use it

Use this to explain *why* an address looks suspicious in human terms — the evidence objects list the actual counterparty addresses and the matched counts. Run `POST /jobs/ingest` for the address first; without persisted counterparties, every detector returns `null`.

## Example

```bash
curl 'http://localhost:8080/heuristics?address=0xd8dA6BF26964aF9D7eEd9e03E53415D37aA96045&chain_id=1' \
-H 'X-API-Version: 1'
```

## Notes

Heuristics are independent — getting one match does not affect another. `null` for every field is the most common outcome for ordinary addresses and is not an error.

parameters.name: address
parameters.description:
Origin wallet address to evaluate. Detection runs against the address
itself and its direct counterparties already persisted in the repo.
Trigger ingestion first via `POST /jobs/ingest` if the address has not
been seen yet.

parameters.name: chain_id
parameters.description: Chain ID. Defaults to 1 (Ethereum mainnet).

200.description: Per-heuristic detection results. Every field is either a [`HeuristicEvidenceDto`] with the matched counterparties and confidence band, or `null` when the pattern did not fire. An all-null response is normal for an unremarkable address.

400.description: Address failed to parse, or `chain_id` is unknown.

500.description: Database read failure while running detectors. The error message is in the body.

400.description: Invalid address or parameters

parameters.name: id
parameters.description: Job UUID

200.description: Current job status

post.description:
Tag an address with a category + label (admin only).

## What this does

Resolves the target entity using the following priority:

1. If the address is already attached to an entity, reuse that entity.
2. Else, if `label_name` matches an existing `(category, label_name)` pair, append the address there.
3. Else, create a brand-new entity with the supplied category + label.

The resulting entity is then persisted with the address attached. This is how you build up multi-address entities (e.g. all of an exchange's hot wallets) by repeatedly labelling individual addresses with the same `label_name`.

Writes go to PostgreSQL. The label is immediately visible to `/score`, `/sanctions`, and the trace-sink classifier.

## When to use it

Use this to inject your own attribution data — internal investigations, curated allow-lists, or imports from third-party providers. For batch imports, prefer `POST /labels/bulk`.

## Example

```bash
curl -X POST 'http://localhost:8080/labels' \
-H 'X-API-Version: 1' \
-H 'X-Admin-Api-Key: <admin-key>' \
-H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
-d '{
"address": "0xd8dA6BF26964aF9D7eEd9e03E53415D37aA96045",
"chain_id": 1,
"category": "exchange",
"label_name": "Binance hot wallet 14",
"label_source": "manual"
}'
```

## Notes

Requires the `X-Admin-Api-Key` header. `risk_score` is auto-defaulted from `category` when omitted — explicit overrides only matter for fine-tuning.

200.description: The resulting entity after upsert. `addresses` includes every address attached to this entity (not just the one you just added).

400.description: Address failed to parse for the chain family, `chain_id` is unknown, or `category` is not one of the allowed values.

200.description: Bulk import result

get.description:
Look up the entity attached to an address (admin only).

## What this does

Returns the entity that contains the supplied address, including every sibling address attached to that same entity. Useful for discovering related wallets — labelling one of an exchange's hot wallets exposes the full set.

This is a DB-only read. No chain RPC is touched.

## When to use it

Call this to render the label / category / risk score for an address in a UI, or to expand a known address into the wider set of co-labelled siblings.

## Example

```bash
curl 'http://localhost:8080/labels/0xd8dA6BF26964aF9D7eEd9e03E53415D37aA96045' \
-H 'X-API-Version: 1' \
-H 'X-Admin-Api-Key: <admin-key>'
```

## Notes

Requires the `X-Admin-Api-Key` header. The path parameter is parsed against Ethereum mainnet — for other chains, attach labels via the same address format that the chain registry produces.

parameters.name: addr
parameters.description: Address in hex (EVM) or canonical chain form.

200.description: The entity. `addresses` includes every address inside the same entity, not just the one queried.

400.description: Path parameter failed to parse as an address, OR the address has no label attached (the endpoint returns 400 — not 404 — for that case).

delete.description:
Detach an address from its entity (admin only).

## What this does

Removes the address from whatever entity currently holds it. The entity itself is preserved (with its remaining addresses) so other addresses in the same entity keep their label. If the address has no label attached, the call is a no-op.

## When to use it

Use this when a labelling decision turns out to be wrong, or when an address is decommissioned. To delete an entity entirely, remove every address one by one — there is no separate "delete entity" endpoint.

## Example

```bash
curl -X DELETE 'http://localhost:8080/labels/0xd8dA6BF26964aF9D7eEd9e03E53415D37aA96045' \
-H 'X-API-Version: 1' \
-H 'X-Admin-Api-Key: <admin-key>'
```

## Notes

Requires the `X-Admin-Api-Key` header.

parameters.name: addr
parameters.description: Address in hex (EVM) or canonical chain form.

200.description: Body: `{"removed": true, "entity_id": "...", "remaining": N}` when the address was attached and is now detached (`remaining` is the number of addresses still on the entity); or `{"removed": false}` when the address had no label.

400.description: Path parameter failed to parse as an address.

get.description:
Find the shortest transfer chain between two addresses inside the persisted graph.

## What this does

Builds a bounded BFS subgraph anchored at `from` (up to `max_depth` hops and `max_nodes` distinct addresses), then runs a shortest-path search ending at `to`. Returns the sequence of transfer edges that connects the two addresses, or an empty `not_found` response when no such chain exists inside the budget.

The subgraph is read only from PostgreSQL; this endpoint never contacts a chain RPC. The path is shortest by hop count — it is not weighted by amount, time, or risk.

## When to use it

Useful for AML investigations of the form "is there a chain of transfers from wallet A to wallet B within N hops?". Run `POST /jobs/ingest` for `from` first; if the chain you are looking for fans out wide, also pre-ingest `to` and bump `max_depth` / `max_nodes`.

## Example

```bash
curl 'http://localhost:8080/path?from=0xd8dA6BF26964aF9D7eEd9e03E53415D37aA96045&to=0x742d35Cc6634C0532925a3b844Bc9e7595f0bEb1&chain_id=1&max_depth=4' \
-H 'X-API-Version: 1'
```

## Notes

A `not_found: true` response does NOT prove the addresses are disconnected on chain — it only means no path was reachable inside the `max_depth` / `max_nodes` budget you set. Increasing the budget can change the answer.

parameters.name: from
parameters.description: Source address.

parameters.name: to
parameters.description: Target address.

parameters.name: chain_id
parameters.description: Chain ID. Defaults to 1 (Ethereum mainnet).

parameters.name: max_depth
parameters.description: BFS depth limit for the auxiliary graph (default 3, see /graph).

parameters.name: max_nodes
parameters.description: Node cap (default 500).

200.description: Shortest path inside the bounded subgraph. When `not_found` is `true`, `edges` is empty and `length` is `0`. When a path is found, `edges` lists the transfers in traversal order from `from` to `to`.

400.description: Either address failed to parse for the chain family, or `chain_id` is unknown.

500.description: Database read failure while building the subgraph. The error message is included in the body.

get.description:
Check whether an address is on any configured sanctions list.

## What this does

Looks the address up against every sanctions list known to the server (OFAC, EU, UN, plus any custom lists you have imported via `/labels` with `category: sanctioned`). Returns a boolean plus, when matched, which list and the entity label.

This is a fast in-memory + DB read; no chain RPC is touched. Sanctions lists are loaded from the entity store on startup and refreshed whenever you POST to `/labels`, so the result is always consistent with the latest admin imports.

## When to use it

Suitable as a compliance gate before accepting an inbound deposit or before sending funds to a counterparty. For high-volume use cases, prefer `POST /sanctions/batch` to amortise round-trips.

## Example

```bash
curl 'http://localhost:8080/sanctions?address=0xd8dA6BF26964aF9D7eEd9e03E53415D37aA96045&chain_id=1' \
-H 'X-API-Version: 1'
```

## Notes

A `false` here only means the exact address is not directly listed. It does NOT prove the address has no exposure to sanctioned funds — use `/score` or `/trace` for indirect exposure.

200.description: Sanctions verdict. `is_sanctioned` is `true` when the address is on a list; `sanction_list` and `label` are populated only on a hit.

400.description: Address failed to parse for the chain family, or `chain_id` is unknown.

500.description: Database read failure while consulting the entity store.

200.description: Sanctions check per address

400.description: Invalid input or batch too large

get.description:
Compute a 0–100 risk score for an address with explainable signals.

## What this does

Aggregates risk signals attached to the address into a single 0–100 score. Inputs are entity labels (from `/labels`), built-in sanctions lists, and the sinks discovered by an internal forward + backward Haircut trace over the persisted graph. Signals are combined using the strategy configured under the `score:` block in `config.yaml` (`max` by default, or `weighted_count` with deduplication).

This is a DB-only read — the chain RPC is never touched. If you score an address that has not been ingested, you will get a low score even when the real on-chain reality says otherwise: ingest first.

## When to use it

After running `POST /jobs/ingest` for the address, call this whenever you need a single risk number plus explainable evidence. Surface the `signals` array verbatim in your UI so investigators can audit each contributor.

## Example

```bash
curl 'http://localhost:8080/score?address=0xd8dA6BF26964aF9D7eEd9e03E53415D37aA96045&chain_id=1' \
-H 'X-API-Version: 1'
```

## Notes

The score is cached for a short, configurable TTL (`risk_cache:` block in `config.yaml`), so back-to-back calls return the same result without redoing the trace.

200.description: Risk report containing the aggregate score and every signal that contributed to it. An empty `signals` array with `score: 0` means the address looks clean to the model — verify ingestion actually covered it before drawing conclusions.

400.description: Address could not be parsed for the chain family, or `chain_id` is unknown.

500.description: Database or internal scoring failure. The error message is included in the body.

200.description: Risk report per address

400.description: Invalid input or batch too large

parameters.name: min_significance
parameters.description:
Per-edge forensic-significance threshold (0..1). Edges scoring below
this are skipped during traversal. Omit to disable the filter.

200.description: Fund trace result with paths and terminal sinks

400.description: Invalid address or parameter value

get.description:
Return the full watchlist (admin only).

## What this does

Reads every entry currently in the watchlist table and returns them as a flat array. There is no pagination — the watchlist is an admin tool expected to stay small (typically tens to low hundreds of entries).

## When to use it

Use this to audit which addresses are currently being monitored. Pair with `GET /alerts` to see which of these have actually triggered.

## Example

```bash
curl 'http://localhost:8080/watchlist' \
-H 'X-API-Version: 1' \
-H 'X-Admin-Api-Key: <admin-key>'
```

## Notes

Requires the `X-Admin-Api-Key` header (or the regular `X-Api-Key` when no admin key is configured).

200.description: Every address currently on the watchlist. May be an empty array.

post.description:
Add an address to the watchlist (admin only).

## What this does

Persists the address in the watchlist table. From this point on, any ingestion that touches the address — whether it was the explicit ingest target or just a counterparty discovered during BFS — automatically writes an entry to `/alerts` recording the triggering transfer.

This is a DB-only write. The address itself does not need to have been ingested previously; you can pre-watch an address and the alerts will start appearing on the next ingestion run.

## When to use it

Use this for proactive monitoring — for example, watch a sanctioned wallet so any future contact with it shows up in `/alerts` without you having to re-score every address you ingest.

## Example

```bash
curl -X POST 'http://localhost:8080/watchlist' \
-H 'X-API-Version: 1' \
-H 'X-Admin-Api-Key: <admin-key>' \
-H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
-d '{"address": "0xd8dA6BF26964aF9D7eEd9e03E53415D37aA96045", "chain_id": 1, "reason": "incident #42"}'
```

## Notes

Requires the `X-Admin-Api-Key` header (or the regular `X-Api-Key` when no admin key is configured). Adding an address that already exists is a no-op.

200.description: Address persisted in the watchlist. The response echoes the canonical address and reason for easy confirmation.

400.description: Address failed to parse for the chain family or `chain_id` is unknown.

delete.description:
Remove an address from the watchlist (admin only).

## What this does

Deletes the watchlist row for the supplied address, if any. Existing alerts already in the `/alerts` log are not touched — only future ingestions stop raising new alerts for this address.

## When to use it

Use this when an incident is closed or a counterparty has been cleared. The audit trail (existing alerts) is preserved.

## Example

```bash
curl -X DELETE 'http://localhost:8080/watchlist?address=0xd8dA6BF26964aF9D7eEd9e03E53415D37aA96045&chain_id=1' \
-H 'X-API-Version: 1' \
-H 'X-Admin-Api-Key: <admin-key>'
```

## Notes

Requires the `X-Admin-Api-Key` header. Removing an address that is not on the watchlist is not an error — the response indicates whether anything was actually removed.

200.description: Body: `{"removed": true|false}`. `true` means a row was deleted; `false` means the address was not on the watchlist (and the call was a no-op).

400.description: Address failed to parse for the chain family.

AddressKindRequest.description:
Request body for `POST /address/{addr}/kind`. Overrides the address-kind
classification stored for an address.

chain_id.description: Chain id the address belongs to. Defaults to `1` (Ethereum mainnet).

kind.description:
New kind to assign. One of: `eoa` (externally-owned account),
`contract` (smart contract / TRX contract), `known_service` (named
custodial wallet — exchange, payment processor, etc.), or `unknown`
(forget any prior classification).

service_name.description:
Display name for `known_service`. Ignored for other kinds. Required
(and non-empty) when `kind == "known_service"`.

AddressKindResponse.description:
Address-kind classification, returned by both
`GET /address/{addr}/kind` and `POST /address/{addr}/kind`.

kind.description:
Current classification. One of `eoa`, `contract`, `known_service`,
`unknown`. `unknown` is returned for addresses the classifier has not
yet seen.

service_name.description:
Service name when `kind == "known_service"`. `null` for every other
kind.

AlertDto.description:
One alert raised when a watched address was touched by an ingestion run.

Returned in the array body of `GET /alerts`. Acts as an audit log entry —
it records the specific transfer that triggered the alert.

address.description: Canonical form of the watched address that was hit.

created_at.description: ISO-8601 timestamp (UTC) when the alert was recorded.

reason.description:
Reason copied from the watchlist entry (`WatchlistAddRequest.reason`)
at the moment the alert was raised. `null` if no reason was set.

tx_hash.description:
Transaction hash that touched the watched address, hex-encoded without
the `0x` prefix.

tx_idx.description:
Log / event index inside the transaction. Disambiguates multiple
transfers in the same tx (e.g. several ERC-20 transfers in one call).

ChainDto.description:
Metadata for a single chain known to the server's chain registry.

Returned as part of [`ChainsResponse`] by `GET /chains`. `source_registered`
tells you whether the server has a live data source configured for this chain
(without one, ingestion and live reads will fail for that chain even though
the chain itself is recognised by the registry).

address_encoding.description:
On-the-wire address encoding. One of: `hex20` (EVM 20-byte hex),
`tron_base58_check`, `bech32`, `base58`.

address_model.description:
Address ledger model. One of: `account` (account-balance model, EVM/Tron/Solana)
or `utxo` (Bitcoin-style unspent outputs).

confirmation_depth.description:
Number of blocks the chain considers "final" for confirmation purposes.
Used by ingestion to wait out reorgs.

id.description: Numeric chain id (e.g. `1` for Ethereum mainnet, `728126428` for Tron mainnet).

native_decimals.description: Number of decimal places for the native asset (18 for ETH, 6 for TRX).

native_symbol.description: Symbol of the chain's native asset (e.g. `"ETH"`, `"TRX"`).

source_registered.description:
`true` when the server has a live chain source registered for this chain
and ingestion/reads are operational. `false` means the chain is in the
registry but no source is configured — ingestion will fail.

ChainsResponse.description: Envelope returned by `GET /chains`. Lists every chain the server knows about.

chains.description:
All chains in the registry, in registry order. Includes both chains
with a live source (`source_registered == true`) and chains that are
recognised but not currently operational.

ClusterResponse.description:
Union-Find clustering result for an address, returned by `GET /cluster`.

A "component" is a set of addresses the detectors believe belong to the
same real-world owner. The queried address is always in the first component
(`components[0]`); additional components describe satellite clusters
discovered during traversal.

address.description: Canonical form of the queried address.

components.description:
Connected components produced by Union-Find over the outputs of every
cluster-formation detector (fan-in/out, peeling, smurfing, etc.). The
queried address is guaranteed to be in `components[0]`. Each inner
`Vec` is the list of canonical addresses inside that component.

EdgeDto.description:
A single transfer edge in the on-chain graph returned by `GET /graph`.

One `EdgeDto` corresponds to exactly one value transfer (a native ETH/TRX
move, an ERC-20/TRC-20 token transfer, an internal contract call, a gas fee,
or a UTXO edge for Bitcoin-family chains). `from` always lost the value and
`to` always received it.

block.description: Block height at which the transfer was mined.

chain_id.description: Numeric chain id this edge belongs to (echoed from the request).

contract.description:
Token contract address when `kind == "token"`. `null` for every other
kind.

decimals.description:
Token decimals used by `formatted` (18 for ETH and most ERC-20 tokens,
6 for USDC/USDT).

formatted.description:
Human-friendly amount: `raw / 10^decimals`, truncated to at most 8
fractional digits with trailing zeros stripped.

from.description: Canonical sender address (the one that lost the value).

index.description:
Log / event index within the transaction. `0` for a native top-level
transfer; higher values disambiguate multiple token transfers inside the
same tx.

kind.description:
Transfer kind. One of: `native` (chain's native asset), `token`
(ERC-20 / TRC-20), `internal` (internal contract-call value move),
`fee` (gas / transaction fee), `utxo_edge` (Bitcoin-family edge).

raw.description:
Raw on-chain amount as a base-10 big integer string, with no decimal
shift applied. For ETH this is wei; for an ERC-20 it is in the token's
own base units.

symbol.description:
Asset symbol for the edge — token symbol for ERC-20 / TRC-20 transfers,
otherwise the chain's native asset symbol (`ETH`, `TRX`, ...). May be
empty for tokens whose contract did not expose a symbol.

to.description: Canonical recipient address (the one that gained the value).

ts.description: Unix timestamp of the block in seconds (UTC).

tx_hash.description: Transaction hash, hex-encoded without the `0x` prefix (64 chars on EVM).

EdgeScoreDto.description:
One edge with its forensic-significance score, returned inside
[`EdgeSignificanceResponse`].

The `score` field is the heuristic significance of this specific transfer
relative to its neighbours — higher values indicate transfers that stand
out and probably deserve human attention.

amount.description:
Raw on-chain amount in the asset's base units, as a base-10 big integer
string (wei for ETH).

asset.description: Asset identifier — chain native symbol or ERC-20 token symbol.

score.description:
Forensic-significance score in roughly `[0.0, 1.0]`. The higher, the
more this edge stands out from its neighbours. Use as a sort key when
triaging large graphs.

timestamp.description: ISO-8601 timestamp of the block, UTC.

usd_value.description:
Best-effort USD value at the time of the transfer. `null` when no
price was available.

EdgeSignificanceResponse.description:
Top forensic edges around an address, returned by `GET /edges/significance`.

The edges are sorted from highest-significance to lowest. Sort order is
stable for ties in score.

address.description: Canonical form of the queried address (echoes the request).

edges.description:
Top edges in/out of the address, sorted by descending `score`. Length
is capped at the request's `limit` (default 50).

GraphPage.description:
One page of a paginated transfer graph, returned by `GET /graph`.

Pagination is over `edges` only — the `nodes` array is global to the request
and only ships on page 0 to avoid duplicating it across pages. Pages are
returned in stable repository order; missing data means ingestion has not
run yet (see `POST /jobs/ingest`).

has_next.description:
Convenience flag: `true` when a strictly higher `page` value still
returns edges.

nodes.description:
All unique addresses in the graph, in deterministic order. Populated
only on `page == 0`; on subsequent pages this array is empty to keep
each page payload bounded.

page.description:
Current page index, zero-based. Always equals the requested `page`
parameter (or 0 when omitted).

page_size.description:
Number of edges per page, after clamping to the server's bounds
(default 100, max 1000).

total_edges.description: Total number of transfers (edges) in the full graph, across all pages.

total_nodes.description:
Total number of unique addresses (nodes) in the full graph, across all
pages.

total_pages.description: Total number of pages available for this request.

HeuristicEvidenceDto.description:
Evidence captured when a single behavioural-pattern heuristic fires for an
address. Returned as the value of any non-null field on
[`HeuristicsResponse`].

The shape of `addresses` and the contents of `notes` depend on which
heuristic fired — see the field docs.

addresses.description:
Addresses involved in the pattern.
* `FanOut`: distinct receivers seen in the burst window.
* `FanIn`: distinct senders seen in the burst window.
* `SmurfingCycle`: `[distributor, ...intermediaries..., cash_out]`.

notes.description: Human-readable explanation including the matched counts and windows.
notes.example: 5 unique receivers within a 86400s window from 0x…

HeuristicsResponse.description:
Aggregated heuristic-detection report for one address, returned by
`GET /heuristics`.

Each behavioural detector runs independently against the persisted
transfers around the subject and reports a non-null evidence object when it
fires. `null` means the pattern did not match (the default and most common
outcome).

address.description: Echoes the canonical form of the queried address.

oneOf.description:
Deposit-address reuse — many distinct senders fund the same single
deposit address (exchange-style routing).

Dwell-time pass-through — median delay between matched in/out pairs
is below `dwell_max_secs` over ≥ `dwell_min_pairs` matched pairs.

Fan-in evidence — many distinct senders converging into one
address inside a sliding time window. `null` if not detected.

Fan-out evidence — one source distributing funds to many distinct
receivers inside a sliding time window. `null` if not detected.

Fixed-amount clustering — ≥ `fixed_amount_min_count` transfers around the
address share the same USD bucket (`fixed_amount_bucket_usd` granularity).

Peeling chain — small slices of inflow being peeled off into a chain of
short-lived addresses.

Smurfing cycle — fan-out followed by intermediaries that route funds
back into a single cash-out address within `smurf_window` and
`smurf_max_depth` hops. `null` if not detected.

Temporal burst — anomalous concentration of transfers (≥ `burst_min_count`
in a `burst_window`, with the burst ≥ `burst_multiplier` × median baseline).

LabelRequest.description:
Request body for `POST /labels` (single) and each element of
`POST /labels/bulk`. Attaches a category + label to an address, creating
or extending the underlying entity.

address.description:
Address to tag. EVM addresses use the `0x...` hex form; other families
use their canonical chain form.

category.description:
Entity category. One of: `exchange`, `mixer`, `bridge`, `defi`, `scam`,
`gambling`, `darknet`, `mining`, `sanctioned`, `unknown`. Drives the
default `risk_score` and the trace-sink classifier.

chain_id.description: Chain id the address belongs to. Defaults to `1` (Ethereum mainnet).

label_name.description:
Human-readable display name for the entity (e.g. `"Binance hot wallet 14"`).
When the same `(category, label_name)` already exists, the address is
appended to that entity instead of creating a new one — this is how
you group multiple addresses under the same logical entity.

label_source.description:
Provenance of the label. One of: `manual`, `chainalysis`, `internal`,
`community`. Defaults to `manual`. Affects nothing automatic — purely
metadata for audit.

label_url.description:
Optional reference URL (e.g. an Etherscan link, an internal investigation
ticket). Stored as-is and round-tripped in `LabelResponse.label_url`.

sanction_list.description:
Specific sanctions list. Only meaningful when `category == "sanctioned"`.
One of `ofac`, `eu`, `un`, or a custom snake_case identifier.

LabelResponse.description:
Entity record returned by every `/labels` and `/entities` endpoint.

One entity may carry many addresses (e.g. an exchange's hot-wallet set),
hence `addresses` is plural. Reading a single address through `GET /labels/{addr}`
returns the entity it belongs to together with all sibling addresses.

addresses.description:
Every address attached to this entity, in canonical form. Includes
addresses other than the one queried — that's how you discover related
addresses by labelling one of them.

entity_id.description: Internal UUID of the entity. Stable across address additions/removals.

label_source.description:
Provenance of the label. One of `manual`, `chainalysis`, `internal`,
`community`. `null` if no label has ever been attached.

risk_score.description:
Entity-level risk score 0–100. Used by `/score` and the trace-sink
classifier.

LabelsBulkResponse.description:
Result of `POST /labels/bulk`. Summarises how many entries were applied
successfully and which ones failed.

errors.description:
Per-row error messages for entries that could not be applied. Format
is `"<address>: <reason>"`. Empty when every entry succeeded.

upserted.description: Number of entries that were upserted successfully.

PathDto.description:
One end-to-end traced path from an origin address to a sink.

Returned inside [`TraceResponse::paths`]. Each path is a chain of hops; this
DTO captures the path's shape (depth, hop count) and its taint summary
rather than every individual edge.

depth.description: Path depth (== `hops`). Number of edges along the path.

destination.description:
Canonical address where the path ends (a sink). `null` for paths that
were truncated before reaching a sink.

hops.description: Number of transfers (hops) in the path.

origin.description:
Canonical address where the path starts (the traced subject for forward
traces, or the source for backward traces). `null` for synthetic paths
that have no concrete starting address.

taint_ratio.description: Taint ratio at the destination of the path (0.0–1.0).

tainted_amount.description:
Raw tainted amount carried along this specific path, as a base-10 big
integer string.

PathEdgeDto.description:
A single transfer edge used to describe a hop in a shortest-path result.

Returned inside [`PathResponse`] by `GET /path`. The edges appear in
traversal order from `from` to `to`.

amount.description:
Raw on-chain amount as a base-10 big integer string, in the asset's
base units (wei for ETH).

asset.description:
Asset identifier — chain native symbol (e.g. `"ETH"`) or token symbol
for ERC-20 transfers.

timestamp.description: ISO-8601 timestamp of the block, in UTC.

tx_hash.description: Transaction hash, hex-encoded without `0x` (64 chars on EVM).

usd_value.description:
Best-effort USD value at the time of the transfer. `null` when no
price was available for this asset/block.

PathResponse.description:
Shortest-path result returned by `GET /path`.

Describes a single chain of edges from `from` to `to` discovered inside a
bounded BFS subgraph anchored at `from`. When `not_found` is `true` the
other fields fall back to neutral defaults.

edges.description: Hops in order from source to target. Empty when `not_found` is `true`.

length.description: Number of hops (== length of `edges`). 0 means `from == to`.

not_found.description:
`true` when no path was found inside the bounded subgraph. When `true`,
`edges` is empty and `length` is 0. A `true` here does not mean no path
exists on-chain — only that none was reachable within the configured
`max_depth` / `max_nodes` budget.

SanctionsResponse.description:
Sanctions-screening result returned by `GET /sanctions` and each item of
`POST /sanctions/batch`.

Combines OFAC / EU / UN list checks with the server's internal entity
labels. A `true` here covers both direct sanctions hits and sanctioned-by-
inheritance cases (e.g. an address attached to a sanctioned entity).

address.description: Canonical form of the screened address.

is_sanctioned.description: `true` if the address appears on any configured sanctions list.

label.description:
Best-known entity label for the address (e.g. `"Tornado Cash"`,
`"Lazarus Group"`). `null` when no label is attached.

sanction_list.description:
Which list flagged the address. One of `ofac`, `eu`, `un`, or a custom
snake_case identifier for non-standard lists. `null` when
`is_sanctioned` is `false`.

ScoreResponse.description:
Risk-score report for a single address, returned by `GET /score` and as
each item of `POST /score/batch`.

The overall `score` is the aggregation of every signal in `signals` using
the `score.aggregation_strategy` configured server-side (`max` or
`weighted_count`). Signals come from entity labels (`/labels`) and from
taint-trace sink classification — run `POST /jobs/ingest` first if the
address has no persisted history.

address.description: Canonical form of the scored address (echoes the request).

chain_id.description: Numeric chain id this score applies to.

generated_at.description:
ISO-8601 timestamp (UTC) when the report was computed. Useful for cache
invalidation in a client.

score.description: Aggregate risk score in the range 0–100. Higher means riskier.

signals.description:
Every individual signal that contributed to the score. May be empty when
the address is clean (in which case `score` is 0 and `is_high_risk` is
`false`).

SignalDto.description:
One contributing risk signal inside a [`ScoreResponse`].

Each signal is a single, explainable reason why the address received the
score it did — for example direct exposure to a sanctioned counterparty,
or a mixer interaction. Severities combine into the aggregate `score`.

description.description:
Human-readable explanation of what triggered this signal — useful to
surface verbatim in an investigator UI.

severity.description:
Severity in the range 0–100. `>= 70` is considered HIGH, `>= 90` is
CRITICAL. The overall `score` is derived from these severities using
the configured aggregation strategy.

SinkDto.description:
A terminal "sink" where tainted funds came to rest during a trace.

Returned inside [`TraceResponse::sinks`]. A sink is the last address on a
traced path where the taint stops moving (because the funds were cashed
out, mixed, bridged, or simply held). The `kind` and `risk_score` come from
entity labels and built-in classifiers.

formatted.description:
Decimal-shifted tainted amount, truncated to at most 8 fractional
digits with trailing zeros stripped.

kind.description:
Sink category. One of: `exchange`, `bridge`, `mixer`, `sanctioned`,
`darknet`, `unresolved`. `unresolved` means the sink could not be
classified from labels.

name.description:
Human name for the sink when `kind == "exchange"` (e.g. `"Binance"`).
`null` for every other kind.

risk_score.description:
Risk score 0–100 attached to the sink itself. Higher means
investigating this sink should be prioritised.

taint_ratio.description:
Fraction of the sink's incoming funds that came from the tainted
origin (0.0–1.0). `1.0` means the sink received only tainted funds on
the observed paths.

tainted_amount.description:
Raw tainted amount that reached this sink, as a base-10 big integer
string in the asset's base units (wei for ETH).

TraceResponse.description:
Result of a fund-flow trace, returned by `GET /trace`.

Combines aggregate statistics, the set of terminal sinks reached by the
taint, and a list of the actual paths discovered. The same sink may be the
endpoint of several paths.

paths.description: Origin-to-sink paths in the order they were discovered.

sinks.description: Terminal sinks reached by tainted funds, deduplicated by address.

stats.description: How much work the trace did and whether it was truncated.

TraceStatsDto.description:
Aggregate statistics describing how much work a trace did.

Returned inside [`TraceResponse`]. Useful for diagnosing under-/over-budget
traces: when `truncated` is `true`, the trace stopped because it hit a
limit, not because it ran out of paths.

addresses_visited.description: Number of unique addresses the trace visited during traversal.

depth_reached.description: Deepest hop reached by the BFS. Always `<= max_hops` from the request.

paths_found.description: Number of complete origin-to-sink paths discovered.

transfers_evaluated.description:
Total number of transfer edges the trace evaluated. Higher than
`addresses_visited` because each address has multiple in/out edges.

truncated.description:
`true` when the trace hit `max_hops`, `max_addresses`, or the internal
`max_paths` cap. When `true`, the result is a lower bound on what's
actually reachable.

WatchlistAddRequest.description:
Request body for `POST /watchlist`. Adds a single address to the watchlist
so future ingestions automatically raise an alert when they touch it.

address.description: Address to watch. Must parse against the chosen `chain_id`.

chain_id.description: Chain id the address belongs to. Defaults to `1` (Ethereum mainnet).

reason.description:
Free-form note explaining why this address is on the watchlist. Surfaces
on every alert that this address triggers. Optional.

WatchlistEntryDto.description:
One entry in the watchlist. Returned by `POST /watchlist` (single) and
`GET /watchlist` (array).

address.description: Canonical form of the watched address.

reason.description:
The note attached when the address was added (echo of
`WatchlistAddRequest.reason`). `null` when no reason was supplied.

api_key.name: X-Api-Key
api_key.description: Required only when server.api_key is configured. Bearer-token form (Authorization: Bearer <key>) is also accepted.

api_version.name: X-API-Version
api_version.description: Required for every request. Supported value: `1`.

tags.name: Graph
tags.description: Build and read the transfer graph. `POST /jobs/ingest` populates the DB asynchronously; `GET /graph` reads it back paginated.

tags.name: Risk
tags.description: Risk scoring, sanctions screening, fund tracing, and behavioural heuristics. Read from the DB only — run /jobs/ingest first if data isn't there yet.

tags.name: Labels
tags.description: Admin-only entity / label CRUD. Use this to attach OFAC, exchange, mixer, darknet, or other attribution data to addresses; the risk model and the trace sink classifier consume those labels.

tags.name: Watchlist
tags.description: Admin-only watchlist of addresses. When a saved ingestion touches a watched address, an Alert is recorded automatically.

tags.name: Alerts
tags.description: Read-only stream of triggered watchlist alerts (audit log).

tags.name: Jobs
tags.description: Asynchronous ingestion jobs. Submit with POST, poll with GET — the worker runs the BFS and chain-source fetch off the request path.

tags.name: Chains
tags.description: Supported blockchain registry (chain id, family, address encoding, native asset).

tags.name: Discovery
tags.description: Service discovery: which chains have a live chain source registered, what each one can do.
